title: Object-Format Transition, Compatibility, and Disaster Defense · Graph Lens: Gita Disaster Recovery Runbook
summary: An organization must prepare for Git 3.0 defaults and SHA-256 repositories while recovering a damaged mirror, validating tooling, and preserving collaboration across object formats. Planned artifact: Add and verify the gita disaster recovery runbook increment in Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Triage ref, object, pack, index, worktree, remote, credential, and artifact damage; preserve evidence; rebuild from trusted copies; and verify exact losses.
Inventory scripts, parsers, databases, APIs, hooks, CI, hosting, and documentation for Git 3.0 breaking defaults, long IDs, and version-gated behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-advanced cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Predict Gita Disaster Recovery Runbook
steps.instruction:
theory case — Case G15 is a theory in a disposable repository. the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs. The learner must predict format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats, run git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output, explain storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract, test this changed condition: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain, and preserve this recovery boundary: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Gita Disaster Recovery Runbook contract for the supplied data.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Locate repository and operation state, compare working tree, index, refs, and graph, preserve a recovery reference, preview the smallest change, then verify both tree behavior and history topology. Apply that move specifically to Gita Disaster Recovery Runbook while producing Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Case G15 is a worked example in a disposable repository. the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs. The learner must predict format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats, run git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output, explain storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract, test this changed condition: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain, and preserve this recovery boundary: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting. Competency probe: Triage ref, object, pack, index, worktree, remote, credential, and artifact damage; preserve evidence; rebuild from trusted copies; and verify exact losses. A defensible solution applies Gita Disaster Recovery Runbook, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to Git 3.0 readiness and disaster-recovery defense.

content.code:
# Worked focus: gita-disaster-recovery-runbook — Triage ref, object, pack, index, worktree, remote, credential, and artifact damage; preserve evidence; rebuild from trusted copies; and verify exact losses.
# Advanced Git 2.55 deterministic scenario 2
# before-ref: main=a11ce00 candidate=b00b1e5
# hypothesis: the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs
git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output
# expected: format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats
# observation: storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract
# changed-case: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain
# after-ref: main=a11ce00 candidate=b00b1e5
# tree-proof: compare the exact affected tree and paths before judging success
# behavior-proof: retain a changed-case test or invariant that can falsify the Git-only conclusion
# recovery: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting
# transfer-limit: object-format migration and Git 3.0 rollout require current hosting and tool compatibility trials
content.caption: Runnable repository-state trace example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case

steps.title: Build Gita Disaster Recovery Runbook evidence
steps.instruction: Append an Advanced Git evidence block headed "# Evidence: gita-disaster-recovery-runbook-15yghp" with exact before and after refs; a falsifiable prediction; the bounded simulator command; expected, observed, tree, behavior, changed-case, and recovery evidence; and one honest real-repository transfer limit.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Gita Disaster Recovery Runbook into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: gita-disaster-recovery-runbook-15yghp
# scenario-key: trace-15yghp probe-phgy51 boundary-yghp15 owner-15yghpx fault-15yghpz
# before-ref: main=a11ce15 candidate=b00b1hp
# hypothesis: the repository stores SHA-256 objects while one integration still assumes 40-character IDs
git rev-parse --show-object-format=storage,input,output
# expected: format evidence separates storage identity from accepted input and displayed output formats
# observation: storage reports sha256 and the audit parser fails its fixed-width object-ID contract
# changed-case: restore a bundle into a fresh same-format repository and reject an incompatible toolchain
# after-ref: main=a11ce15 candidate=c0ffeep
# tree-proof: the affected tree and path set match the predicted repository transition
# behavior-proof: a changed input preserves the named product or release invariant
# recovery: preserve trusted refs, object stores, translation data, manifests, and exact loss accounting
# transfer-limit: object-format migration and Git 3.0 rollout require current hosting and tool compatibility trials
